data.title: Glencore - Attractive Mining/Metals At The Right Price (But Not Now)
data.description:
When Glencore (GLNCY) and Rio Tinto (RIO) first started talking about merging their operations, I had long and thorough plans to initiate coverage on both tickers and cover the merger in detail. However, once I'd gotten some of my valuation models off the ground, the news came out that the companies abandoned merger talks about a month back. What it seems is that the merger talks couldn't get past the valuation stage and leadership (not surprising, given the history and the structure/fundamentals of both companies). This hasn't exactly been the first time this has been attempted, and Glencore PLC has made it clear that it's not the last time that it may (potentially) want this.

Scale and efficiency in this business is obviously very important. The merger would mark incredible scaling opportunities for both businesses. It would be one of, if not potentially the largest merger in mining history, with valuations talked about at about $260-$300B+. It is to be noted here that while Glencore has been making comments, it's actually Rio Tinto that is by far the larger business of the two.

As a result of the failed merger, Glencore fell by over 5% on the open market. As a value-oriented investor that likes buying quality assets cheaply, this renewed my interest in both Glencore and Rio, and I decided that despite the failed merger, I would initiate coverage on Glencore. I've been expanding my metal coverage (mining also) the last few months, reflecting not only an expanding diversification of my coverage in terms of geography (you will note more LATAM companies and Brazilian ones), but also opening up sectors (more mining overall).

It's important that investors understand that these are incredibly cyclical end markets. These end markets make or break the short-term and even medium-term realities of these companies' valuations. In order to invest in them, you need to understand that you may be in the negative or flat returns for years. This does not mean you did anything wrong, or the company did anything wrong. It simply means that the company's end markets are, at the time, prohibitive.

But as I hope that I have showcased in many of my articles, it's only a question of time before quality companies with underlying fundamentals revert to fair or above-fair value. The danger instead I see people doing is investing in companies that are neither safe nor have good fundamentals, but act as though they do, and argue for potential TAM, PEG factors or forward growth factors as though these equate to historically sound or currently sound results.

They do not.

So with that in mind, here's what I think of Glencore PLC.

Glencore PLC - A mining giant with great upside at a good valuation

In mining/metals, there are two sides to the business. You have on the one hand, the mining business, and then you have the global commodities business. Glencore does both. It not only successfully at an industrial scale, mines key metals of copper, cobalt, and zinc, it also markets these products, which means it captures efficiencies and margins usually lost between different players in the mining/metals supply chain. It also provides the company and its employees with a massive amount of expertise, and its tradition comes with a deep understanding of the market.

This alone, if coupled with a fundamentally sound balance sheet, is enough to pique my interest as a value-oriented investor.

What in turn differs Glencore from more "pure" miners, is that Glencore also operates its assets with a marketing business. It therefore has the ability to monetize its own production. As self-evident as this business model may sound to you, dear reader, it's not usually the case.

Why?

Capital management reasons. Doing both requires mixing capital-intensive asset management business with low-margin trading. Usually, the two are considered very difficult to combine. Miners tend to avoid it because of its risk/reward and complexity. Glencore PLC has grown with this model, and has both taken its "hits" for doing it (like investigations into bribery/corruption). As a comparison, Rio is a pure-play miner - it can focus solely on capital allocation, management, and ESG. It's a simplification. As it is with Glencore, it's one of the company's strengths.

Fundamentals, meanwhile, are a mixed bag. On one hand, it's safe with solid assets and good profit - on another level, the recent results have been impacted by rising debt (though from a very low level) and net profit losses. Glencore has some exposure to coal still, and this has been going negatively, with a 1H25 trading drop of over 75%. Furthermore, the company has been forced to take large write-offs. 2024 saw a big one exceeding $10B in total combined receivables and impairment expenses related in part to coal, in part to asset valuations.

I would relate the company's recent cyclicality to completely "natural" shifts for a miner and marketer like this. So, key point - understand what you are buying.

Overall, I believe Glencore PLC is in for a very good 2026. This is due to strong pricing for the company's key products, which are copper, cobalt, and nickel, as well as one of the key moves for Glencore over the past few years - a move away from the weakening coal sector. To clarify, we're talking moving away from thermal coal, while the company will still maintain its focus on coal used for the making of steel, which is likely to continue being priced highly.

This upside is already, I believe, somewhat confirmed in 2026E with a strong share price recovery from 2025 levels. The company is combining operational efficiencies and savings with strong cash generation efforts.

It's worth mentioning that, unlike many US-based miners and commodity players, the notion of a dividend tradition isn't of central importance to Glencore. The company, to confirm this, suspended its distributions during the pandemic but has since returned to them. It should be evident from my recent articles and coverage that while I find dividends to be of central importance, it's not a disqualifier if the company either doesn't have them, or chooses not to do them. It's analyzed on a case-by-case basis.

Glencore's native trading ticker is GLEN, a London-based ticker. The company carries a BBB+ rating and a current native yield of close to 2.5%. The way that the company is viewed by analysts today is a significant ramp-up of earnings in 2026E, with a stabilization of the current level for both 2027 and 2028E, with slower growth of single digits (albeit high-level single digits).

The problem or challenge given by analysts following Glencore in its current position of £5-£6/share is that the valuation, even after the drop following the no-merger news, is far too high for what the company carries in terms of risk from its business model and from the volatile end markets. This risk is best showcased in its lack of historical forecast accuracy.

As you can see here, the likelihood of company performance and valuation (at certain levels) needs to be understood in the context of this volatility. Anyone arguing that Glencore PLC is going in the direction X, needs to also qualify the risk for -X. I argue that Glencore generally is reversing in 2026E - but there is quite a bit of risk "baked into" this, obviously. It can be argued that the company's share price has not at all normalized from its merger talk assumptions. Consider for a moment that less than 1.5 years ago, it was trading at less than half of what it is today. Furthermore, the at this point relatively tired argument (to me, not necessarily the market) of "AI and electrification" growth is one I consider significantly overplayed.

I do believe that the company's assets are undervalued by the market on a historical basis, at very least the copper assets - but this obviously doesn't mean the company should trade at "any" valuation.

Those looking at Glencore as an investment should understand that the company's capital allocation for 2026-2028E will be primarily aimed at internal growth. A significant spike in the dividend payment is therefore an unlikely event, in my view. Consider also that volume guidance for 2026E is actually, across most parts, lower or on par with the previous year, especially Zinc. It's therefore fair to say that the company's upside will be dictated by pricing more than by volume, as well as savings. Thankfully, the company is estimating significant reductions in unit costs over the next few years, which should work to eliminate or at least lower volume pressure.

Moving to valuation

Valuation - While a great company, I unfortunately view it as overvalued

It should be clear from my wording that I view this company as attractive from a conservative perspective. At the right price, I'd invest. Unfortunately, the current valuation and multiples dictate that this is not a suitable time for investment.

The company's volatility and earnings metrics suggest a mix between P/E and EBITDA multiples, while also making individual adjustments where necessary for NAV valuation for individual assets. The current company average P/E is close to 30x. This is impacted by poor results between 2023-2025 - but even working from a 2027-2028E valuation, with upsides considered, the current multiple is close to 20x. This is higher than Rio Tinto is today. Of particular worry to me on an operational side is the 2024 results - poor despite a favorable market. DCF analysis suggests, based on conservative risk premiums, that the stock is anywhere from 20-30% overvalued above £5/share. I don't assume this based on the common argument that the trading division is a net risk rather than a benefit, but production issues, asset quality, and valuation (the copper production for Glencore peaked in 2014 and has mostly declined since), and a residual reliance on legacy thermal coal (which admittedly is lowering).

Counter-arguments is the rising copper potential the company is currently in. However, the rising reliance on Copper is in itself a risk I want to price for, given the common "energy transition" argument, where the visibility is actually worse than investors believe (in my view). Suggestions that there is a massive deficit incoming are countered by pricing for commodities triggering substitutions (already happening - aluminum is being used), recycling (which several of my covered companies do), and the hollow nature of the China transition argument. I believe the increase in demand from China will not be able to meet the decline from China due to its property market declines. That's beside the point that I believe the Green/AI demand is overstated.

So I take a somewhat more balanced and conservative approach to valuation.

Any used valuation metric is going to be incredibly volatile. Glencore's EBITDA multiple has a 5-year range of 3.3x to over 10x. The P/E goes from less than 11x to more than 30x. There is, unfortunately, no safe or remotely accurate way to measure or target this company. Best practice is all I can offer you. A range. I will say that market valuation targets for Glencore are atypically high, with a consensus on a premium, with a backdrop of 19 analysts that for the most part, consider the company to be a "BUY".

However, a reason or justification why we should pay a NAV premium 30%+ above that seen in 2024A, beyond the usual argument that its assets are undervalued, is not one I have found. It's also interesting that despite a gold in its mix, the company's NAV is now substantially above industry averages. Majors trade at around 0.7-0.9x typically - gold at below 0.8x, with the current valuation of 1.24x implying extremely high-quality assets. I believe this is too optimistic given the closure of assets (Mount Isa, Lady Loretta, Koniambo, Rhovan Vanadium, Mokala, Bracemac-Mcleod). The mentioned ones are all assets that are nearing LoM (life of mine) and have closed.

I therefore view this as too optimistic. Starting off with NAV, I would put a fair value at £4/share, which is about 1x (and that is still high). In terms of EBITDA, I would move to the upper average, at 6.5x. Based on a 2025E EBITDA of $13.5B and a 6.5x multiple, net of debt, we get an EV of $76.55B. With the SO of 11.29B, that comes to about £6.4/share. Similar for P/E. If I average the company to the rest of the industry, and use a conservative 2028E forecast for earnings, the implied P/E at 15-17x comes to between £ 4.5- £4.5-£6/share. I therefore believe this to be a suitable range for the business, with £6/share marking the upper range.

Averaging this, I give the company a fair value of £5.3/share.

However, that means that in order to get a 15% annualized RoR, and that's assuming you're reinvesting the dividends, you'll need to buy at below £4/share, near £3.9/share native, to be exact.

The PT is therefore £3.9/share.

Thesis

Glencore is a very solid mining/commodities marketing company with a lot of upside at the right price. Despite regulatory and operational risks, I very much like what I see and would like to buy the company at a good price. Recent price surges, however, have made the company somewhat uninteresting. I believe the company is trading at too high multiple. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of £5.3/share, which in turn comes to a £3.9/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized with dividends.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

This company is overall qualitative.This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.This company pays a well-covered dividend.This company is currently cheap.This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of £3.9/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 BDCs With 'Safe' Yields That Are About To Fool Everyone
data.description:
Although many BDCs already have adjusted their dividends to the downside, the current discounts we see in the sector send a clear message that there is more pain on the horizon (including dividend cuts).

Meanwhile, I think that a lot of investors have still anchored their expectations around BDC income stability.

The key driver for the BDC dividend cuts so far (over the past couple of quarters) has been the lower interest rate environment (i.e., cuts by the Fed).

Going forward, exactly this aspect was assumed to force the majority of remaining BDCs to cut their distributions as well.

However, the recent Fed meeting and the recalibrated interest rate cut expectations by the market indicate that we might face a higher-for-longer scenario.

Theoretically, this means that some BDCs might be able to provide dividends higher for longer as well.

For example, here I would include well-established BDCs, which as of Q4 2025 had covered base dividends, robust loan investment portfolio statistics, and below-sector-average non-accruals.

Yet, I'm afraid that a decent chunk of such BDCs that look solid and have not cut dividends yet will most likely announce lower dividends as we move closer to year-end (irrespective of when the next rate cuts happen).

Namely, damage from the previous interest rate cuts has already been done, and some (even solid) BDCs are still behind the curve in terms of aligning their dividends with the new reality.

In the article below I will now share two BDCs on which I hold constructive views, but, in all fairness, I think that quite many investors are not expecting dividend cuts.

#1: OBDC, 13.2% yield

Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) is the third-largest publicly traded BDC, which has preserved its quarterly base dividend (and even steadily increased it) since the IPO back in 2019.

Even though OBDC has a direct relationship with Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), which has received bad press lately (e.g., failed merger between OBDC and OBDC II, liquidity gating), the underlying fundamentals and business performance are in great shape.

Because of that (great fundamentals), some investors might consider the current dividend safe and fixed going forward.

For example, OBDC ended Q4 2025 with a non-accrual rate of 1.3% (based on FV), which is well below the sector average. The portfolio itself is well-diversified, first-lien-focused, and saturated with companies that have net LTVs below 45% and weighted average EBITDA profiles of around $237 million.

However, there are simply just too many headwinds for OBDC's NII generation to sustain the current dividend.

In the chart below we can see how the spread compression remains an issue as OBDC's portfolio continues to get churned out from the loans, which were underwritten when the overall spread levels in the industry were very high:

This, in combination with lagged effects from the Q4 2025 interest rate cuts, should introduce a downward pressure for Q1 2026 NII per share results.

And it is not good news given where the current base dividend coverage stands - i.e., there is no margin of safety left.

In fact, if we factor in PIK income, which accounts for ~10% of total income, the current dividend falls in a negative cash coverage zone. Moreover, it is worth noting that the Q4 2025 NII per share result included a $0.02 per share positive one-off item, which most likely won't be present in Q1 2026 data.

Finally, I think that the CEO in the Q4 2025 earnings call did not send the strongest message pertaining to OBDC's dividend stability in the near term:

However, we will continue to discuss this carefully with our Board and evaluate the dividend each quarter, particularly as the full effect of these lower rates and spreads are now impacting the portfolio. While lower rates and tighter spreads will compress asset yields and NII returns across the industry, they generally improve borrower fundamentals and in turn, credit quality. Against that backdrop and given the solid borrower performance we continue to see, we do not expect broad-based credit issues in our portfolio.

My base case is that OBDC cuts the dividend already in Q1 2026 (when the earnings deck gets circulated to the public).

#2: BXSL, 12.6%

Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) has a similar story to OBDC.

Namely, the underlying fundamentals are robust, but the direction of travel for its NII per share generation is negative, which, in turn, renders the current dividend unsustainable.

In fact, I would even argue that BXSL has a stronger portfolio and better credit risk protection than OBDC.

In Q4 2025, the non-accrual rate came in at only 0.5% (on an FV basis). The portfolio-wide company LTM revenue and EBITDA levels were both up by around 10%.

Just as for OBDC, the portfolio is skewed towards first-lien loans, which are underwritten for well-established businesses that generate significant EBITDA figures. Because of its scale (fourth largest publicly traded BDC), BXSL is able to strongly diversify the portfolio (e.g., there are 316 companies, with the largest one accounting for ~2% of the total loan book).

It is also encouraging to see that the board has approved a $250 million buyback program, which sends a positive signal about management's confidence in the underlying portfolio and its performance.

However, still with all of this in mind, I doubt that BXSL will be able to maintain the current dividend for long.

Based on Q4 2025 results, the base dividend coverage stands at 104% - i.e., very little room for an error.

Theoretically, if BXSL managed to deliver growth, then the issue might be resolved, but I doubt that this will be possible.

For example, BXSL ended Q4 with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.30x, which means that securing positive net funding volumes is challenging, to say the least. Typically, BDCs tend to avoid breaching the 1.25x level, as there the financial risks start to increase exponentially. I wouldn't be surprised if BXSL started to deleverage a bit, which obviously is not positive to the NII per share generation.

Speaking of leverage or outstanding borrowings, BXSL has $1.4 billion of ~3.2% fixed-rate debt refinancings this year (at higher cost of debt levels), which will depress the NII per share even further.

On top of that, BXSL has the same issues as OBDC regarding PIK exposure (8% of total income), the lagged effects from lower base rates, and headwinds from tighter spreads.

My base case here is that BXSL adjusts its dividend later in 2026 (with Q2 or Q3 2026 earnings reports) simply because it still has a slight NII surplus over the current dividend.

The bottom line

The fact that incremental base rate cuts are unlikely to materialize in the next couple of quarters does not mean that current BDC dividends are safe.

Even if we put aside the potential manifestation of credit risks (i.e., increasing non-accruals), those BDCs that have not yet normalized their dividends are likely to do so in the foreseeable future.

However, I would like to make it clear that SOFR-driven dividend cuts are not necessarily value-destructive. It is completely logical that the lower benchmark (risk-free) rates push yields down for most assets out there, including private credit assets, which have significant floating loan investment portfolios.

Here is a relevant (explanatory) comment from Sixth Street Specialty Lending, Inc. (TSLX) management:

To be clear, the dividend cuts that have started to proliferate across the BDC sector over the past few quarters have nothing to do with credit losses. The loss rates for private credit have been relatively stable. But the math will tell you the sector cannot fund current dividends at the spreads at which it has been deploying capital.

Finally, let's remember that the average base dividend cut in the sector so far has been around 20%, which for OBDC and BXSL would imply an adjusted yield of 10.6% and 10.1%, respectively.

In my humble opinion, both OBDC and BXSL are solid income producing vehicles despite the elevated probability of dividend cuts.

The most important thing is that their non-accruals remain stable. Otherwise, if they start to go up, we will be talking about credit risk driven dividend cuts rather than SOFR-driven, which in that case would cause a significant selloff, like for, say, FS KKR Capital (FSK) and Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN).

See more details on OBDC and BXSL in my ticker-specific (bullish) articles here and here.

data.title: How To Create A Wheel Strategy With Sector ETFs To Generate Income
data.description:
I write a lot on Seeking Alpha about options-based funds, but I tend not to invest in many of them myself. There are a handful that have made their way into my portfolio, but for the most part, I prefer to manage my options positions myself. The control and precision one can have is unparalleled to what comes with options funds. I find that my ability to be nimble creates its own alpha; I am not moving billions of dollars at a time, so I can move in ways they cannot.

One strategy that has become very popular not only for its flexibility but also for its ability to generate income is a method often called “The Wheel” or a “Rotational Strategy.”

There are many DIY investors allured by income ETFs but not impressed by their results who could benefit from learning this strategy and implementing it in their portfolios. This strategy aims to solve that by providing a constant income source while allowing for timely and precise entries and exits from long stock positions.

The appeal of this framework is that it can be easily systematized, comes with an easy-to-follow flowchart, and can do that while adding a heavy income stream to a portfolio. It turns patience into income and alpha, rewarding those who are willing to consistently work the cycle.

The Wheel Strategy

The trade dynamics are fairly straightforward, revolving around a “lot” or 100 shares of a stock or fund. The strategy requires investors to have the cash on hand to purchase at least 100 shares of their target asset.

This is when some of the newer “mini” versions of popular ETFs can come in handy for investors with smaller accounts, as it's easier to gather the funds for 100 shares of the State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM) or the iShares Gold Trust Micro ETF (IAUM) than SPY or IAU. Just beware that their options chains are often less liquid than their pricier, but heavier on volume, counterparts. This may mean worse spreads but also fewer expiration dates, e.g., monthly vs. weekly.

Entry: Cash-Secured Puts

Investors typically start with a cash-secured put (“CSP”), where they sell a put on the asset they would like to own. Selling a put gives the investor the obligation to buy the asset at a certain price, the strike they choose. This is essentially setting an entry price for the asset. I frequently see this behavior from commenters and analysts alike (of which I am also guilty), where they will say, “I'm going to wait until the stock hits $50 before I buy.” This is a perfect time to start this strategy, setting the $50 strike as the entry point.

Once expiration comes, there are two most likely outcomes (not accounting for the risk of being exercised early, etc.):

  1. The asset price is above the strike. The put expires worthless, and the investor keeps the premium they were paid for selling it.
  2. The asset price is below the strike. The investor is assigned 100 shares priced at the selected strike (e.g., 50p = $50).

In the first scenario, the investor can roll the put out another period (week, month, etc.) and start the process again to collect another round of premium or deploy the capital elsewhere. This is what is likely to happen most of the time, assuming you are selecting prudent strikes that are not breached too often.

Exit: Covered Calls

In the second scenario, where the investor is assigned shares, they begin the second half of the strategy: covered calls ("CC"). Now that they have entered the long position, they will choose an exit by selecting the strike they want to sell at. This falls along the same lines as setting price targets — at what level you are willing to exit the position. Investors will take their lot of shares and sell CC against it, collecting income while they wait for the position to rise to the strike they selected.

Once expiration comes, there are two most likely outcomes (not accounting for the risk of being exercised early, etc.):

  1. The asset price is above the strike. The investor's shares are called away at the new strike.
  2. The asset price is below the strike, and the call expires worthless. Premium is kept.

If the call expires worthless, then it can be rolled out to the next expiration period, and the process repeated.

Once shares are called away, the investor is free to start the process over again, using the cash from the called shares to sell a new put.

The Wheel Visualized

Here's a flowchart that may help visual learners:

The goal is essentially to always be collecting income and using the sources of income (CSPs and CCs) to time entries and exits from positions.

If one is willing to hold cash while they wait for a stock or an ETF to hit a certain level, why not be paid while you wait? The same applies to those holding stock waiting to hit a certain price target. Both sides of the wheel produce income and feed into each other, so the process can be repeated on the same ticker ad infinitum or to move between tickers as a rotational strategy.

To demonstrate, I'll use SPYM as an example.

An investor starts with enough capital to buy 100 shares of SPYM.

The investor sells-to-open ("STO") the 75p.

I picked the 75 strike because it is at 30 delta, offering reasonable risk to reward, but investors will need to decide on their own selection criteria.

They put up $7,500 in collateral (which can be held in interest-bearing instruments like money markets, T-bills, etc. at the same time).

They are paid $125 in premium, generating a ~1.67% return for the month, or 22.32% annualized.

Note: Don't expect put premiums to be this high consistently in the future; selling equity puts is very lucrative right now due to the ongoing U.S.-Iran War at the time of writing.

On April 17, the investor will have the above profit and loss structure, either being assigned 100 shares at $75/each or being able to freely roll out the put to a new strike in June.

Here is what the flipside looks like if you started with shares already and were going to work the call side for income.

An investor starts with 100 shares of SPYM, with a cost basis of $75.

Investor Sell to Open the 81 strike call. Again — picked because it is the closest to 30 delta for our purposes here.

They hold the shares of SPYM in the meantime, gaining the returns (positive or negative) of the shares, up to the strike they chose.

They are paid $73 in premium, generating a 0.97% or 12.28% annualized.

On April 17, the investor will have the above profit and loss structure, either having their shares called away at $81/each or being able to freely roll out the call to a new strike in June.

And they can repeat the cycle either way.

Expressed together, they can have a P&L structure that looks like this:

Because of the strategy's systematic nature, it's easy to turn it into a spreadsheet to track your "adjusted cost basis," or how much premium you have earned while you wait to be assigned on either side of the equation.

Applications of The Wheel Strategy

Now that we understand the basics of how to run the strategy, we can get creative about how it is applied.

Regular readers of mine will know that I am a fan of sector ETFs and regularly use them to express my convictions about changes in macro, technical levels, etc. They can be very versatile tools in this way.

Consider a strategy involving the rotation of two defensive sectors, like consumer staples (XLP) and utilities (XLU). Investors could sell a CSP on XLP two months out, then the following month, sell a CSP on XLU for a similar length. This gives investors one defensive position to roll every month, while also leaving a lot of room for income collection and diversity between the two sectors.

From there, investors can move up the risk spectrum, pairing higher volatility funds like technology (XLK) and consumer discretionary (XLY). Higher volatility means a higher premium, but also a higher likelihood of being called at any given time. There are trade-offs to consider.

Investors could consider a barbell approach as well, pairing a low-volatility and high-volatility sector. For example, they could open both an XLP and XLK wheel, aiming for predictable assignment during business cycles, while the other aims for increasing overall portfolio yield at the expense of more full wheel cycles (assigned put shares that are later called away).

Combinations are endless here. An investor who is cautious about the economy could rotate between XLU and XLK. One who is expecting a cyclical rebound might prefer industrials (XLI) and financials (XLF), which tend to correlate. Energy (XLE) and materials (XLB) also fall into this pattern, allowing investors to express their opinions about the market without concentrating exposure to one sector.

Investors should consider the volatility of each sector and how they can balance income and risk.

This could also be done with diversified asset classes, for example. Consider an investor who balances two wheels, one on equities via SPYM and one on gold via IAUM. During bull markets, investors will rotate through equities but likely spend most of the time selling puts and catching secular downturns as times to buy in, while holding and selling calls against gold. When that paradigm flips in a market crash or panic, the gold may get called and the equity shares assigned. This seems to flip the wheel on its head, but investors have really just done a tactical rotation. Not only that, they've been consistently collecting premiums while they wait for that moment.

Because this investor with SPYM and IAUM runs the wheel regardless of which part of the cycle they are in, they run a barbell that allocates between stocks and gold tactically while earning income on the volatility of both assets.

The same kind of rotation could be done between small caps (IWM) and large caps with SPYM, enabling investors to rotate between the two and harvest the highest volatility and risk from small caps while still participating in their cycles against large caps.

Strategic Strike Selection

Every investor has their own criteria for when to buy and sell, and that can be fluid, which makes strike selection difficult. While I tend to use my macro convictions to decide on which assets I want to own, I find it difficult to use as a guide for specific entries and exits.

Here is where I tend to turn to technicals. Put strikes are best when they align with support levels, and call strikes are best when they align with resistance levels. This is not only where the implied volatility is concentrated and priced in, making it more lucrative to target these levels, but also where the asset is most likely to respect and stay rangebound. When assets break their range, that's when an investor would expect the wheel to begin spinning.

Consider a few assets that have been benefitting from the ongoing market rotation (a perfect scenario for this strategy has been unfolding around us), which have suddenly clammed up since the onset of the war with Iran. They are now looking ripe for put selling, including small caps, communications (XLC), and industrials.

This is where investors can really let their technical expertise shine.

A more systematic approach would be to decide on a certain delta value or percentage OTM. An investor may consider selling 5% OTM puts and calls to provide a buffer and give a known and predictable upside to the given leg of the wheel. This is similar to how some passively managed covered call funds work, like QYLD, but only on the call side; they usually target 2-3% OTM.

And just as with all things, there are folks who prefer to rely on fundamentals. Consider how the various instruments are trading compared to their histories.

Risks of The Wheel Strategy

The primary risk, outside of being called early and having to manage your positions at irregular times (rare, but it happens), includes poor strike selection. If assignment never happens, that could mean that investors collect premiums but miss out on gains in the underlying. The goal is to capture both, but that is far easier said than done.

An investor can lock themselves into an endless chase with this strategy if they are not careful to remember that assignment is one of the goals and should be similarly prioritized with income collection.

On both the put and call side, investors can also find themselves underwater if they are put shares that continue to fall and fall. If they sell a call with a strike below their purchase price, they could end that spin of the wheel at a net loss. If they sell calls too high, they collect too little income. In a prolonged drawdown, this strategy can clamp up its income engine or force losses.

This is true with any ownership strategy, truthfully, and is why I wanted to introduce ideas like rotating between assets and defensive/offensive sectors. When applied to a singular index or stocks, the strategy can be helpful for portfolio returns. When applied as a framework to monetize patience and build a portfolio at the structural level, it's powerful for returns. It's a different level, but it requires the consistency in application and patience to wait out the cycles.

Conclusion

The goal of running the Wheel strategy is to create a system that rewards you for being patient and expressing your willingness to enter and exit a position at certain levels. When markets trade within ranges, wheel investors tend to outperform buy-and-hold.

But investors who sell puts to enter their positions and calls to exit them routinely could generate serious portfolio income, with my examples on the mini S&P 500 ETF showing the potential for 10-20% annualized returns if executed right. That last part will require good timing and prudent decision making, which is why technicals and fundamentals are still our friends.

Remember that ultimately, this is a strategy about entering and exiting positions, not income generation outright. That is part of it, but a wheel that is never assigned is one that is likely underperforming in the long run because its strikes are too conservative. Ownership is the goal, but so is paying the right price.

Good luck if you try this strategy for your portfolio.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Aviva: Strong Results And Capital Returns Support Continued Upside
data.description:
Considering the current market environment, we remain positive on Aviva's (AIVAF) (AVVIY) share price performance (Fig. 1). Since early December 2025, Aviva's share price has been up by 5% vs a negative S&P return. Here at the Lab, we continue to prefer Aviva and L&G (LGGNY) over other UK life insurers, including M&G, Phoenix, and Lancashire. Aviva has growth potential and diversification, with attractive income prospects. In our last analysis, we reiterated our buy rating and raised Aviva's target price. This was supported by stronger fundamentals, ongoing transformation, and the Direct Line acquisition. After having analysed Aviva's Q4 and FY results, we continue to see a progressively de-risked investment case.

Fig 1

Aviva Results and Our Upside

Aviva reported solid yearly numbers. The company's operating profit rose by 25% to £2.2 billion. However, adjusting for Direct Line's acquisition, Aviva's EBIT growth was at 15% (still very solid). A positive note is that Aviva beat Wall Street's operating profit forecast at £2 billion. The company showed healthy momentum in our view. Going down to the P&L, EPS was up 17% to 56p with a return on equity of 17.5%. Looking at the division, Aviva's P&C (general insurance) business continued to drive over half of the division's operating profit, thanks to gross written premiums up 16% year-on-year. The company also increased its marginality with a better combined ratio at 94.6%. This was 1.7 basis points lower than 2024. The supportive results in general insurance were partly offset by a softer-than-anticipated performance in the IWR segment.

Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

Following the 2025 FY release, Aviva is still targeting EPS growth of around 11% between 2026 and 2028 (Fig. 3). The numbers benefited from better-than-forecast weather. Still, Aviva's EPS growth exceeds the expected 6-10% for large-cap EU insurers (Allianz (ALIZF) at 10%, Zurich (ZURVY) at 9%, AXA (AXAHY) in a range between 6-8%). Someone could argue that the company looks inexpensive, but once adjusting for a lower solvency ratio, the implied adjusted P/E might be fair. However, over the next five years, we expect Aviva to strengthen its Solvency II ratio to above 200% gradually. This is aligned with the EU sector average, and is well supported by the company's net capital generation after dividend payments and buybacks. We believe that the market is not pricing Aviva's valuation currently.

Fig 3

Still related to the company's solid capital generation profile over the medium term, here at the Lab, we forecast a share buyback increase of at least £350 million from 2027 onward. This is likely to be communicated alongside the 2026 results. Under this assumption, Aviva would offer an all-in 2026 yield of roughly 7%, compared with a peer average of around 6%. This is also supported by the recent 10% DPS hike (Fig. 4). Our team believes the company might sustain a higher payout ratio of around 85%. This is due to Aviva's strong cash-generation characteristics and its retirement segment.

Fig 4

In recent years, Aviva acquired strategic core businesses in UK general insurance (Direct Line and Probitas), UK wealth (AXA, Azur and Succession Wealth), UK protection (AIG), and Canadian speciality lines (Optiom). Despite that, the company has a solid balance sheet. More importantly, Aviva's balance sheet also appears relatively resilient to credit stress. The company offers one of the lowest sensitivities to credit events among peers (Fig. 5). For example, we provide AXA's Solvency II sensitivity to a +50 basis points in credit spread (Fig. 6), and its Solvency II ratio declined by 1 basis point, whereas it is 0 for Aviva. This was well supported by the Direct Line acquisition, which helped to reduce the overall volatility. This is also true for Zurich, as shown on page 52 of its annual presentation. Of course, the headlines were not positive about the evolution of Shareholder Solvency II. The company reported a -23 basis-point annual impact from the acquisition, but it remained at 180% and looked healthy (Fig. 2).

Fig 5

Fig 6

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Here at the Lab, we like Aviva's resilience with cash remittances above £2 billion and earnings diversification. The company has set a credible combined ratio evolution to 94% in 2026. In Aviva's estimates, the company expects large losses in the commercial line but supportive cost synergies from the Direct Line acquisition. Additionally, the company is outperforming in motor new business, with +1% vs. a soft market. Considering its 2025 EPS (56p), we decided to increase our EPS projection from 10% to 11%. This led Aviva to a 2026 EPS of 62.16p, which is well aligned with its internal estimates. Applying a 12x P/E, we reached a valuation of 745.92p. 1 ADR = 2 Aviva ordinary shares, so we derive a target price in ADR of $20 per share. This implied a 20% capital appreciation opportunity (and does not include the lower share from the proposed buyback). The sector median P/E backs our valuation (Fig. 6) and is well supported by double-digit EPS growth. Additionally, this is combined with a dividend yield of almost 7% (DPS of 43.2p) and a buyback yield of 1.8%. Therefore, we remain buyers.

Fig 6

Risks

We have long covered Aviva's downside risks, so we report integration risks from the latest acquisitions and negative sentiment about a lower Solvency II ratio. That said, Solvency II debt leverage reached 30.1%, in line with guidance. This was also up from 28.9%, including Direct Line. Additionally, we expect the company to build its solvency towards 200% over the years (excluding dividends and share buybacks). The company may be affected by higher comps, which could trigger a softening in pricing. As an insurance player, Aviva is exposed to changes in the equity market. Additionally, any corporate tax changes, accounting changes, and increased regulatory requirements might impact its P&L evolution and, consequently, its stock price performance.

Conclusion

In a nutshell, Aviva is delivering on its plan. We expect earnings to surprise to the upside, and it's important to note that Aviva's numbers are now more aligned with those of the EU large insurance players. The company offers a solid EPS growth combined with a supportive total yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FLCH: The Dragon Discounted, But Not Enough
data.description:
In the ever-changing global landscape of 2026, the Franklin FTSE China ETF (FLCH) represents one of the most cost-efficient vehicles for gaining access to the world’s second-largest economy. As China transitions away from its traditional growth strategy based on real estate and toward a high-tech industrial revolution strategy based on "New Quality Productive Forces" leaders, the fund’s underlying index tracks the leaders in this emerging space. Although the overall narrative for China’s equity market is polarized by the geopolitics and deflationary environment, the current valuation gap could represent an attractive mean-reversion play.

This article will focus on the catalysts and risks driving FLCH’s current trajectory in the current environment.

The Fund

Franklin FTSE China ETF's main goal is to provide exposure to a broad range of Chinese companies, ranging from large caps going down to small cap companies. The ETF is designed to track the performance of the FTSE China RIC Capped Index. The index is market cap weighted with a free float adjustment. Seeing as it’s a Regulated Investment Company(RIC) Index, it is imposed certain cap restrictions on its constituents, those are single company cap of 20% of the index and the sum of all constituents with a with above 4.5% cannot exceed 48% of the index. This cap restrictions provides investors a certain diversification benefits, improving its risk profile while also leaving room for more constituents which means increased potential idiosyncratic growth.

Around 75% of the holdings are classified as large cap (above $10 billion), and 17% are mid-cap companies, with a market cap between $2 billion and $10 billion. The total number of constituents of the fund is 996. The fund is not currency hedged, so investors face currency risk.

Regarding the fund itself, the expense ratio is 19 basis points, and it earns a Seeking Alpha A- Expense Grade. Liquidity is relatively high for a retail investor, with an average daily dollar volume on a 3-month basis around $3.39 million and an AUM of $271 million, earning it a Seeking Alpha B- Grade. In terms of the Dividend Grade, it is rated C, mainly because of the low 3-year CAGR being a meager 3.8%, while the dividend yield is 2.42%.

Moving on to the holdings look-through, the fund is almost fully invested in equities, with a relatively diversified sector structure. Consumer cyclical, Communication and Financials make the bulk with a weight of 26.05%, 17.91% and 17.66%, respectively. Technology, Industrials and Basic Materials follow with a weight below 10%.

We’ll compare the fund to other comparative funds using the Seeking Alpha grading system. Other comparative broad China exposure ETFs that I will use in the comparison are iShares China Large-Cap ETF(FXI), iShares MSCI China ETF(MCHI) and State Street SPDR S&P China ETF(GXC).

The fund has generally below average Seeking Alpha grades, except for the Liquidity and Expenses grade which we discussed earlier. Relative to other peer funds, the fund is proportionately ranked with superior grades in the Expenses category, lagging marginally in the liquidity category to FXI and MCHI which are larger funds. All in all, we can say that the fund is comparable to peer funds.

In terms of performance, FLCH has performed in line with peer funds on a short and long-time frame.

Trade Relations, Monetary Policy and China's 15th Five-Year Plan

The most evident catalyst for FLCH is the trajectory of US-China trade relations following a rocky 2025. After the US Supreme Court struck down IEEPA-based tariffs in February 2026, the effective tariff rate on Chinese goods, while still elevated at approximately 30%, has declined meaningfully from peak levels above 100%. A 90-day trade truce reached in mid-2025 was followed by an extension of tariff exclusions on 178 Chinese product categories through November 2026. President Trump's anticipated visit to Beijing presents a near-term catalyst, any signals of a broader trade agreement or further tariff rollbacks could serve as a meaningful positive re-rating event for Chinese equities broadly and FLCH specifically.

On the monetary policy front, the PBOC has reiterated a "moderately loose" stance for 2026, with tools such as the reserve requirement ratio and interest rate cuts to help liquidity needs, stepping up support for domestic demand, technological innovation and SMEs. Recent upticks in inflation even pre-Iran war have certainly dented the PBOC narrative, but in my opinion, the current macroeconomic setup could very well be supportive for monetary easing given the PBOC’s stance and an earlier end to the war.

China’s technology space continues to provide short-term sentiment momentum, with DeepSeek’s open-source AI models having fundamentally changed global perceptions of Chinese technological competitiveness. Alibaba Group Holding Limited's (BABA) Qwen model series was rated as the most downloaded open-source model series on Hugging Face in both 2025 and 2026, while Chinese labs are increasingly becoming more competitive on key benchmarks. This AI re-rating has been a key contributor to FLCH’s strong return in 2025.

China's 15th Five-Year Plan for the period 2026-2030, is arguably the most structurally important long-term catalyst for FLCH. This is because the plan prioritizes "modernizing the industrial system" in industries such as advanced manufacturing technology, artificial intelligence, green energy, and semiconductors at the center of China's growth strategy. Government credit is also being directed towards these industries through structural monetary tools in line with Beijing's "Five Major Articles" framework. This is arguably the most important long-term catalyst for FLCH given its 9.26% allocation to Technology and an 17.91% weight in Communication. Moreover, this is also a period in which state support is combined with actual innovation in AI and robotics for the first time in China's history, making this a unique long-term growth catalyst compared to previous investment cycles driven by real estate and infrastructure.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, FLCH and the Chinese equities market remains attractively valued, with a trailing P/E of 13.85x it trades at a steep discount compared to the Emerging Markets group trading around 17.26x P/E and even more compared to the S&P500’s 26.61x P/E. I would not really call this a bargain given China’s political setup and the regulatory crackdown a few years back, but it certainly is trading at a low valuation at the moment. We can see on the graph that the discrepancies in valuation compared to the MSCI All World index and the Emerging Markets group is really pronounced at this point in time.

Risks

Investing in FLCH is certainly not for the faint of heart due to the geopolitical and regulatory risks involved. The biggest "black swan" event is an unexpected escalation in the Taiwan Strait or a deterioration in trade relations with the US and EU, resulting in further sanctions or delisting threats. At home, while the "regulatory crackdown" threat seems to have diminished in recent years, the "Common Prosperity" push means that the Chinese government can change its mind in an instant, much to the detriment of minority shareholders. The property sector is another slow disaster for China. While the systemic collapse is averted in 2026, the debt overhang continues to dampen the "wealth effect" for the average Chinese household, potentially constraining the upside for the fund's consumer-related components.

Conclusion

FLCH is a diversified play on China with a low fee, which is obviously attractive. On one hand, Chinese policymakers are easing and investing in growth sectors. The themes of economic reform, as well as cheap valuations, could certainly have a positive impact. On the other hand, headwinds in property, debt, trade policy uncertainty, geopolitical turmoil and declining investor sentiment are large. The fund had a great run in 2025, but risks are present and could mean a potential pull-back. Overall, given this mix, I rate FLCH a HOLD.

data.title: GameStop Holiday Quarter Earnings Preview Signals Muted Numbers
data.description:
The brick-and-mortar video game retailer GameStop (GME)—arguably now closer to a cash-rich vehicle than a traditional retailer—is set to report its holiday season quarter on March 24 after the closing bell.

As usual, there's little to no guidance from management to anticipate what may lie ahead. The only assumption is that the top line will probably continue to decline and the business will continue to operate with low profitability, with half of the profits coming from interest on the firm's investments.

In my previous thesis, I argued that its mega-robust cash balance offers a limited downside risk to the thesis, but at the same time contrasts with the lack of sufficient predictability to model an upside case.

Despite recent (unconventional) new events leading GME to outperform the broader market throughout this year, the thesis remains blurry, but arguably, with an implicit optionality, given an unconventional CEO (and largest shareholder) being able to pull a card out of his sleeve at any time.

Even though I see GameStop increasingly trading towards fundamentals and less as a "meme", I maintain a neutral rating before FQ4.

Valuation Anchored in the Balance Sheet

First of all, GameStop shares returned to trading as a "meme", by which I mean soaring without fundamentals, right in early February of this year, after famed investor Michael "Big Short" Burry revealed a bullish position in GME, through a long and fun-to-read thesis on its private Substack account.

From his post, I highlight this specific part“:

“I own GME. I have been buying recently. I expect I am buying at maybe soon 1x tangible book value / 1x net asset value. And getting a young Ryan Cohen investing and deploying the company’s capital and cash flows. Perhaps for the next 50 years.”

It is not too difficult to predict that GME will tend to trade at 1x TBV/1x NAV in the near future, either through price or fundamentals.

Today, GameStop has stockholders' equity of $5.3 billion, zero goodwill and zero intangibles, and a market cap of $10.46 billion, which implies trading at ~1.97x tangible book value. And because there are no relevant intangibles in the balance sheet, net asset value (NAV) is essentially identical to tangible book value, so GME also trades at almost 2x NAV.

Thus, Burry's thesis is that the TBV and NAV multiple will eventually be cut in half, and this can happen in two ways: (1) either the market cap collapses with a strong correction, (2) the denominator (TBV/NAV) will grow materially over time, either by cash retention (and eventually an efficient capital allocation by Ryan Cohen and his team).

In comparative terms, considering that in FY24 (referring to 2023), GME reported a stockholders' equity of $1.34 billion and traded in a range of ~$7 billion market cap, the TBV/NAV multiple exceeded 5x.

But realistically, for GME to trade at 1x TBV/NAV in the near future, it is necessary that, at the same time, the TBV would need to double in value at a constant price.

Obviously, this is a huge leap, and it is very difficult to count on a "Roaring Kitty return" event to make shares skyrocket to the moon again, and with that, raise billions in equity issuances.

There is no visibility that GameStop today can double its TBV via normal operation. Or can it?

Doing the math, anticipating a target of 1x TBV today, there is a gap to be filled of ~$5.16 billion. But in the last twelve months, GameStop generated $583 million in operating cash flows. A meaningful portion of this cash generation is explained by interest income on its ~$8.8 billion cash balance, resulting in $282.5 million of interest income (yield of ~3.4%). Predicting that this cash generation will continue to grow at a conservative pace of 5% per year, it would take 7 to 8 years for the TBV gap to be filled.

The other, faster path is that 1x TBV/NAV divided by the 447.9 million outstanding shares results in a target price of $11.84, at a 49% drop from current prices.

I don't think this is a binary scenario. Either a 50% collapse in GME or TBV doubling in ~6-9 years is not a base case. I believe that the greatest probability would be some compression of multiple and some growth of TBV.

Mr. Burry's insight practically leads me to visualize GameStop's thesis through a lens that the market doesn't need to "break" the stock, and neither does GME need to execute perfectly. All it takes is a slight disappointment and moderate growth (perhaps at the current pace) for GameStop to trade close to the net value of its assets, i.e., when the market is pricing only the balance sheet and stops believing in the balance sheet expansion story.

A Word Ahead of Earnings

There's very limited visibility into what's happening strategically at GameStop.

The sole communication channel that the management team has with investors is through its corporate filings, and more specifically, a breakdown of its retail business objectives with practically three bullet points, and a few more short sentences about short to medium-term expectations.

But as GameStop approaches reporting its FQ4 results, this being the most seasonally important quarter of the year (holiday season), we can outline some points about expectations.

First, revenues will most likely be weaker on a year-over-year basis. In FY25 Q4, GameStop reported $1.28 billion in revenue, already coming from a decrease of 28% versus the holiday quarter of FY24. With ongoing store closures in FY25, cuts in SG&A, and a bigger shift in mix (greater focus on collectibles, the most profitable segment), I think it's unlikely that revenues in 4Q25 will come in much above $1 billion.

On the operating side, assuming gross margins of ~30% (consistent with the last six quarters) and with OpEx falling ~20% sequentially in the last three quarters, I anticipate that we can see expenses of ~$230 million, which would result in an operating profit of ~$70 million, very close to what was seen in the same period last year. Adding an eventual ~$75 million in interest on investments (assuming a yield of 3.4%), GME would report a net income of $108.7 million. This would lead me to estimate a base case scenario that GameStop closes FY25 with a net profit of ~$400 million, somewhere around ~$0.89 EPS.

As one can see, none of these are spectacular numbers. Potentially, they are worthy of a yawn. That's why I believe that on the operational side, only if there is a big surprise in demand—perhaps something coming from collectibles—or that brings margins to levels well above average, it could represent a bullish catalyst for the thesis.

Furthermore, with respect to management team announcements, since FY25 Q2, GameStop has not proposed private offerings of convertible bonds, and since FY25 Q1, it has not updated on new Bitcoin purchases.

It is unpredictable what may be going on in CEO Ryan Cohen's mind regarding these topics, but I would say that, as he himself (and other insiders) have been buying shares and in January disclosed a very aggressive long-term compensation plan, it is plausible that there will be no new dilutive initiatives, after all (1) theoretically there is logic in not diluting one's own stake; (2) dilution goes against the aggressive compensation plan, (3) there is already too much cash; (4) the current valuation context is not at an extreme level.

But of course, none of this is guaranteed. Ryan Cohen, as CEO and investor, has an opportunistic and non-ideological philosophy. If there is hype or a strong rally, there could likely be new issuances. However, with GME having a short interest of ~14.6%, and cost-to-borrow at 0.49% per year, I don't see a short squeeze scenario that can send GME "to the moon" without a specific mega catalyst.

Final Remarks

It is extremely difficult to reach any firm conclusion on GameStop's thesis, both bullish and bearish.

But if I were to bet, I would say that FQ4 results will be unimpressive and will most likely bring almost nothing relevant from the point of view of business fundamentals.

On the other hand, well-known names such as Michael Burry backing GME and its management team give a welcome visibility to the stock's momentum, and also bring attention to the viability of GameStop being an investable vehicle for many who still prefer to avoid looking at the thesis on a more fundamental approach, and continue to see it as an "irrational meme".

I believe that, for those who are willing to bet a very small portion of their capital on a company led by arguably the most unconventional management team among all the companies listed on the stock market, there is definitely optionality in the thesis. And I wouldn't see it as an unreasonable or bad bet.

But from the point of view of an asymmetric investment, it is still very complicated to issue a more constructive opinion, and that's why I see GME as a Hold.

data.title: Fidelity Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Small Cap Value Fund (FCPVX) takes a value approach to investing, focusing on companies with above-average quality characteristics, including good capital allocation, and that are trading at below-market multiples.
  • We strive to buy the best businesses available among these value plays, though in periods of extremely high return variance, we may opportunistically go further down the quality spectrum if there is sufficient valuation dispersion in the market to justify it.
  • Each of our investments is scrutinized for a perceived margin of safety, assessed through rigorous analysis of each firm's income statement and balance sheet.
  • We employ a disciplined portfolio construction approach and manage the portfolio in a relatively concentrated fashion in order to emphasize our best ideas, and to try to have most of the risk and return of the fund driven by stock-specific factors.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s):

Gabriela Kelleher

Trading Symbol:

FCPVX

November 03, 2004

The securities of smaller, less well-known companies can be more volatile than those of larger companies. Value stocks can perform differently than other types of stocks and can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time.

Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Performance Review

The fund's Retail Class shares returned 1.58% for the three months, trailing the 3.26% advance of the Russell 2000® Value Index. Long-term performance comparisons remain quite favorable.

In Q4, equity investors benefited from a generally supportive environment for higher-risk assets, backed by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and two additional interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve following its September move – the first since December 2024. Small-caps, as measured by the Russell 2000® Index, gained 2.19%, modestly trailing the 2.66% advance of the large-cap S&P 500® index, as investor enthusiasm moderated relative to the prior quarter.

Against this backdrop, the fund's bias toward higher-quality, profitable companies detracted during a period when unprofitable businesses meaningfully outperformed. From a sector perspective, picks in materials and financials hurt the most versus the benchmark, followed by positioning within health care. Conversely, security selection in real estate notably contributed.

The biggest individual drag on relative performance was a non-index position in government service and infrastructure provider Parsons (PSN)(-25%). Its shares fell sharply in early December after the firm lost a government contract to modernize the U.S. Federal Aviation Administration. This was disappointing for Parsons, but we believe the firm has many other growth opportunities and that its longer-term prospects remain unchanged. An out-of-benchmark stake in Blue Bird (BLBD)(-18%) also detracted, with expected earnings weakness and persistent cost, tariff and backlog pressures weighing on investor sentiment. Further hampering results was an overweight in Penguin Solutions (PENG)(-26%), which designs and manufactures specialty solutions for the computing, memory and LED markets. Although the company delivered a strong earnings report, it did not raise its full-year financial projections, which led to weakness in the stock – which we found unwarranted. We added to all three holdings this period.

In contrast, contract electronics manufacturer Sanmina (SANM)(+33%) lifted the fund's relative result, as shares benefited from a well-priced corporate acquisition, as well as higher earnings potential stemming from new AI-related business. We trimmed this position considering its reduced upside. Also contributing was a non-index position in Knight-Swift Transportation (KNX)(+33%), a trucking company that benefited from favorable industry pricing trends. Given that we still see meaningful upside in the stock, we added to the portfolio's position. In addition, the fund benefited from an out-of-benchmark stake in real estate broker Compass (+32%), which bounced back from an earlier decline heading into the quarter.

Outlook and Positioning

Our strategy seeks to generate long-term outperformance through individual stock picking, while limiting the impact of other variables. A key part of our approach is to avoid paying too much for a stock in the first place. Second, we look for stocks with what we consider a sufficient margin of safety. Third, we analyze management and other factors to uncover what we think are high-quality firms.

Over the past three months, notable shifts to the portfolio included reducing the fund's exposure to the information technology sector while reinvesting in areas of the market that had lagged and where we see a favorable risk/reward trade-off. These included consumer staples, early-cycle industrial and housing companies. We also reduced the fund's allocation to consumer discretionary stocks after several gained meaningfully since last April, when President Trump announced a delay in implementing higher-than-expected tariffs. Although we believe the risk/reward trade-off in this sector has declined, we remain overweight consumer-related companies given economic stimulus related to the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.

In 2025, the small-cap rally was led by highly speculative, non-earning, low-quality stocks. As a result, valuations for high-quality companies ended the year at levels we believe are especially attractive. In addition, dispersions are high – meaning investors are seeing wide gaps in how the market is valuing different stocks – which we view as a favorable set-up for the future, as fundamentals may begin to matter more. In this environment, I'm hopeful the market may start to broaden out. And if small-caps can return positive earnings growth after several years of declines, I believe they could be positioned for an upward move in their valuations.

In addition, small-cap valuations relative to those of large-caps are at an extreme, while low-quality stocks are expensive – factors we believe are positive for security selection.

With the economy getting a boost from lower interest rates, accelerated depreciation potentially increasing capital spending, a push toward reshoring, and ongoing federal economic stimulus, we anticipate that this favorable backdrop for small-caps could support stronger earnings growth and potential outperformance for the asset class.
